title: Greek Village Bread

overview.description: A rustic Greek village loaf with a crisp crust, chewy crumb and deep wheaty flavour, made with a blend of white and wholemeal flour.

method: Add the flours, yeast, sugar and salt to a large mixing bowl, keeping the salt and yeast separate initially.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms.
Knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl and cover.
Leave to rise until approximately doubled in size, about 1–1½ hours.
Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and shape into a round loaf.
Place the loaf on a sheet of baking paper or a floured proving surface.
Cover and leave to prove for 30–45 minutes.
Meanwhile, preheat the oven to 240°C, or as hot as your oven will reliably go, with a baking stone or heavy baking tray inside.
Score the top of the loaf with a sharp knife.
Transfer the loaf to the hot baking surface.
Reduce the oven temperature to 210°C and continue baking for approximately 25–30 minutes.
The loaf is ready when deeply golden and it sounds hollow when tapped underneath.
Cool completely on a wire rack before slicing.

learning.did-you-know: Horiatiko psomi means village bread and refers to rustic Greek-style bread rather than one single universally standard recipe. Regional versions vary in flour, fermentation and shaping.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a simple flour-and-water dough develops flavour and structure through fermentation, and how a very hot oven creates a crisp crust.
skills: Baking with steam and high heat

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A rustic everyday bread that gives the collection a proper Greek table bread and demonstrates how a simple dough can produce a loaf with substantial flavour and texture.
chapman-tips: The dough should be soft and slightly sticky rather than stiff.
Don't add all the water immediately; flour absorbs different amounts depending on the brand and wholemeal content.
A long enough first rise makes a noticeable difference to the flavour.
A very hot baking surface helps produce a better crust.
For a thicker, chewier crust, place a tray of boiling water in the oven when the bread goes in.
Don't slice the loaf while it is still very hot or the crumb can become gummy.
The loaf does not need to be a perfect circle; a rustic shape is part of its character.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Slice before freezing if you expect to use it a few pieces at a time. Thaw at room temperature or refresh briefly in a hot oven.
notes.leftovers: Use for toast, sandwiches, dipping into olive oil, breadcrumbs or rustic Greek-style bread salads.
